channel.title: The Butlin Visit on Shipwreck, the Rescue -- JMW Turner
channel.description: Recent content in The Butlin Visit on Shipwreck, the Rescue -- JMW Turner

item.title: Chapter 19: The Stage Was Set
item.description:
Shipwreck, the Rescue — full painting

The stage was set. All the efforts of the last several years were to be decided worthy or worthless in one short moment—the time it took to grimace or grin.

Martin Butlin had agreed to come to Canada and assess the painting firsthand. Christie’s senior consultant. The world’s foremost Turner expert. Coming to Penticton.

The painting would face its most important test.

item.title: Chapter 20: The Arrival
item.description:
![Martin Butlin portrait](/images/mb port kaisweb.jpg)

Martin Butlin portrait

Butlin arrived. The weeklong assessment began. Bob and Graham had prepared for years—but nothing could prepare them for the reality of confronting the art world’s establishment.

“We’re always doing little deals,” Butlin would later confess. “One’s got a monopoly; it’s like incest in a way.”

The insider was about to open up—whether he meant to or not.

This is an excerpt. Read the full chapter in Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.

item.title: Chapter 21: Portrait of a Soul
item.description:
Jiří Kayser painting Martin Butlin

The dinner went well. George Kayser had all the time needed to observe his subject. Martin was alive to the moment—animated, anecdotal, occasionally indiscreet.

Over wine and conversation, the guard came down. Stories of Tate Gallery politics, Christie’s inner workings, the art world’s dirty secrets emerged like bubbles from deep water.

Bob and Graham listened. And recorded everything.

This is an excerpt. Read the full chapter in Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.

item.title: Chapter 22: Disrespectful Tomato Indeed
item.description:
![Detail: Turner’s characteristic ‘blot’ technique](/images/blot web.jpg)

Detail: Turner’s characteristic ‘blot’ technique

“The thing is, people can always say, well, you know—if it’s not Turner, who is it?” Martin mused.

Even Martin expressed doubt that anyone younger than the young Turner could have painted Shipwreck, the Rescue. The style, the technique, the fury—all pointed to one hand.

But connoisseurship is subjective. And Martin had his doubts about committing to an opinion.

item.title: Chapter 23: Fury and the Neglected Lover
item.description:
![Infrared imaging reveals underdrawing](/images/swr infra red web.jpg)

Infrared imaging reveals underdrawing

“Put me up in front of a really good defense advocate…” Martin shuddered. “These people are terror!”

Evelyn Joll had been grilled about a valuation at a trial. He said he was never prepared to go through that experience again. Martin understood the feeling.

The legal fears of experts drove them to incompetence. “If you knew, my boy, how much it is the other way round. The interests concerned are so great.

item.title: Chapter 24: Jockeying or Joking
item.description:
Fingerprint evidence documentation

Graham pressed the fingerprint issue. “Why would somebody put a false fingerprint? That’s fraudulent!”

Martin agreed: “That much is clear.” But he remained unconvinced. The evidence was there—the ridge patterns, the pore impressions—but the expert’s mind was closed.

The battle between science and connoisseurship raged on.

This is an excerpt. Read the full chapter in Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.

← Chapter 23: Fury and the Neglected Lover

item.title: Chapter 25: The Only Painting Ever
item.description:
Turner’s signature on the Ehrenbreitstein

“J.C. Robinson—a brilliant connoisseur, whose purchases laid the foundations of richness.” Sir Oliver Millar’s assessment of the Surveyor who once owned the painting.

Robinson had served as Surveyor of the Royal Collection. He had redirected the V&A’s collecting priorities. He had acquired Michelangelo drawings for the British Museum.

And he had owned Shipwreck, the Rescue. The provenance was impeccable.

This is an excerpt. Read the full chapter in Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.

item.title: Chapter 26: Looking Blindly
item.description:
![Close-up: survivors on the spar](/images/spar close web.jpg)

Close-up: survivors on the spar

It was already into the third hour of serious discussion. Bob fell into conversation with Frances Butlin—partly for her ability to carry on a two-way conversation, as an escape.

Graham continued pressing Martin on the stylistic evidence. The brushwork. The composition. The lozenge shape that Turner pioneered.

Martin’s objections were growing thinner. The evidence was overwhelming.

This is an excerpt. Read the full chapter in Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.

item.title: Chapter 27: Loose Lips On Board
item.description:
![Detail: the wrecked ship in the distance](/images/swr4000dpijpeg auto levels adj wreckweb.jpg)

Detail: the wrecked ship in the distance

The winery tour had gone well. Back on the boat, the rocking motion loosened tongues. Martin leaned back and let slip what he’d been holding back.

“We’re all in each other’s pockets—it’s like incest in a way.”

Bob was silenced. The insider had confirmed what they’d suspected: the art world was a closed club, and outsiders need not apply.

item.title: Chapter 28: Backroom Whispers
item.description:
Photogravure: the painting as published in 1903

Peter Watson had written about it: “Christie’s was run by a close-knit coterie.” The relationships between dealers, experts, and institutions formed a web of mutual dependency.

Martin’s candid remarks confirmed the pattern. The art world was not about truth—it was about control.

Bob recorded everything. The evidence was damning.

This is an excerpt. Read the full chapter in Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.

item.title: Chapter 29: The Astonishing Monogram
item.description:
![Hidden signature under varnish](/images/sig and date under varnish on stern of boat - typical location.jpg)

Hidden signature under varnish

Martin spontaneously erupted with another Tate Gallery musing. His life was inextricably tied to the institution—to its paintings, its politics, its power.

The Astonishing Monogram. Turner’s hidden signature patterns. Evidence that had been dismissed for decades was suddenly undeniable.

Martin had to acknowledge what the science proved. But would he?

This is an excerpt. Read the full chapter in Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.

item.title: Chapter 30: Sticky Wicket
item.description:
The Madonna group — mother and child in the rescue boat

The journey to the island was solemn and soggy. The Romantic grandeur of the mountains was hidden in cloud cover.

Martin’s final assessment. The verdict. After a week of debate, evidence, and candid conversation—the moment of truth.

Would he support the painting? Or would politics prevail?

This is an excerpt. Read the full chapter in Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.

item.title: Chapter 31: Farewell or Freedom
item.description:
Turner statue — farewell to the master

“Keeper”—a really old-fashioned term that Martin seemed to cherish beyond reason. It broadly denoted somebody proudly responsible for maintaining the best interests of Nation and Crown.

Martin’s farewell. The painting’s future hung in the balance.

The Butlin visit was over. But the story was just beginning.

This is an excerpt. Read the full chapter in Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.
